## [00:00 - 03:00]

So this lesson is all about team. And I'll tell you, my biggest mistake, if I look back over my business, is probably that I really took a long time to get on board with building a team and with getting any kind of help in my business. It was crazy how understaffed we were and how hard I was working and the few people working with me were working. So this was a big mistake. And I think it, I just, you know, I had some teachers, some gurus that said your life will be a living hell if you have even a single employee. And I just, this person just, that was bad advice. It was just simply bad advice. Now, if you're just starting out, if you're absolutely starting from scratch, starting from the beginning, haven't done your first launch, haven't started building anything yet, then you probably want to skip this video and come back to it later on. But at some point, you're going to be limited into how much you can grow and how much you can build in your business until you start growing a team. And this has been a huge trend, like in my Platinum Plus program and now in my Launch and Club coaching program of, you know, a number of years ago, it wasn't a focus of people in this business. They had the most minimal teams where they just outsourced everything and there was no one building a serious team or very, very, very few people building a serious team. Now it's something that is happening. And frankly, I mean, the businesses, the size of businesses in our world has grown so much. It used to be a business doing $100,000 was sort of at the top end of the market. And now, you know, there's so many businesses doing hundreds of thousands of dollars or millions of dollars or into the tens of millions of dollars. And it's just a natural evolution of this business. And so it is a huge trend to really start growing. And it's something that I've, you know, we've grown up a real team and it's a real amazing team with an incredible culture. And I just wanted to talk a little bit about that path and maybe give you some ideas. So the first thing you want to do is, when you're very, very, very stern now, I think for most people, the first thing you should hire for, and you probably don't necessarily want to hire an employee, but this is probably, could be more of an outsourcing thing. The first couple of areas that to me, most people start with is customer service. You can have all kinds of different fancy memes for that, like client happiness. And I can't tell you all the different names, but it's customer service or client service. And the reality is if you're building a business, you probably don't want to be spending a lot of your time answering customer service emails. But it's very, very important those emails get answered and they get answered very, very well. So this is, I think this is my first hire that we had the first team member and that was Betty. And she's actually still with us.

## [03:00 - 06:01]

It's been over a decade now. In fact, that's a real theme on my team is that pretty much no one ever leaves. And customer service is probably not a super high value thing for you. So these two things are pretty easy to outsource. I outsourced this one for the first decade or so before we brought anyone in-house. Customer service was my first hire. ## [06:01 - 09:02]

And it's got worksheets and all the, everything that goes into the entire course. I just have to stand in front of the camera and teach and my content team takes that and turns it into a course. So that's a really great thing. When I started out, I had to do every single one of those steps and you will too if you're just starting out. But this is where you can get to where you are just dialed in and I'm doing what I'm best at. What I'm best at is not loading up videos and making them look good on the site and taking those videos and turning them into transcripts and making those transcripts read write. Those aren't my unique ability areas standing in front of the camera and teaching is. Then we have, and this took a really long time. So this stuff isn't in order. After these top two, this stuff is not in order. We have a copy team. I used to write every single email, including those emails that said, hey, we've got a coaching call today. Or, you know, hey, the new module is released. Or, hey, we've just released a new video on our blog. So I used to do every single piece of copy, every single piece in the last year or so we have now developed a copy team. So I don't have to do all that copy. I'm still reviewing all the copy. I'm still doing some of the really critical sales copy, but we have a copy team that's now learned how to write copy in my voice. So it sounds like me. Then we have an entire coaching team. To actually, accounting is how you keep scoring business. Again, if you're just starting out, probably not as important. But as you start to grow, it becomes important. ## [12:02 - 15:05]

is this 25-person team, we're virtual. We don't have an office, we're virtual, 100% virtual. Because I live in Durango, which is Durango, Colorado, small town in the mountains in Colorado, there's about 13,000 people here in this town. Tremendous, great town, tremendously talented people here. But some of these skills are pretty specialized. And being virtual allows me to pull from a worldwide talent pool. And so we have people across the country and outside the United States that is part of our virtual team. And then when we tie into these adjunct teams, most of them are virtual as well. So we're working with people all around the country. Now to do that, we're using tools that just were not available a few years ago. Right now, as I record this, and maybe as you're watching it, our tools may have changed, but one of our primary tools is Slack. That's for communications. It's an amazing communications tool. We don't use email. The only email that happens in my business is sometimes we have to email people that are not in the business. People are outside the business. But within the team, there's a lot of communication going on between 25 people, working very hard to get a lot done. There's no email, there's zero email. We're 100% on Slack for our communications. We also use Zoom. So we meet on Zoom and we do video calls so we can see each other and work together on Zoom. We use Google Drive for documents to collaborate on documents. So these three tools allow us to be super productive. We also use something called Teamwork, which is for project management. There are a lot of solutions here. So don't necessarily say, I'm gonna go use Teamwork because Jeff uses it. Take a look. There's things like Trello, Asana, on and on and on. All kinds of tools to manage projects, to share documents. So with these things, we're able to go virtual. Now I'm gonna tell you that virtual isn't the be all and end all. There are some times where I wish we had an in-person office and we were all meeting in the office because there's just inherently, there's some things that are harder to get done when you're virtual. You're waiting on someone and they think they're waiting on you, you're waiting on them. You don't realize it. If you were just in a desk next to each other, you could just look over and say, hey, what's up? When am I gonna get that? And they'd be like, I don't know, I'm waiting on you. And so those type of things happen on a virtual team. So there's pluses and minuses to having a virtual team, but it's working for us, working for me right now. And then finally, the one thing is, I am really clear, this is the key piece. I don't manage these 25 people.

## [15:05 - 17:39]

I am not a manager. Some people are talented managers, not one of my skills, not one of my talents. And frankly, it's not one of the highest and best uses of my time. My time is better sitting here teaching you. My time is better putting together products, putting together marketing, building relationships in the business, building relationships in the industry, finding joint venture partners. That's where my time is better spent. It's not better, managing is not a unique ability. It's not a skill I should be practicing every day. I should be doing these other things that drive the business forward. However, the one thing that I need to be, and you will need to be, if you start to build something like this, again, not for beginners, but if you start to build something like this, you have to be a leader. So I have had to learn to become a leader. I think I've been a great leader, I think, in our industry. I think I've been a phenomenal leader for our PLF owners and the people in the community. But I wasn't born a natural leader for my team. And since about 2014, that has been part of my growth path, is learning how to be a leader. And at some point, you will need to be a leader if you're gonna grow a team. If you don't have to be a manager, but you need to be a leader. You have to set the vision for the company. And you have to lead your leaders, and you have to lead your managers. So that's a little bit about our team, where we are, how we've grown. Like I said, it was a big mistake I made was taking too long to bring people on board. Now I know that there are great people out in the world that they're willing to get on board with my vision. They're willing to get on board with my, with where we're going, with the vision I've got, of raising up an entire generation of entrepreneurs who are gonna go out, be entrepreneur heroes and change the world. So that's what we're doing, we're creating these world-changing entrepreneurs. That's my job. And I've got a lot of people on my team now that said, hey Jeff, I like your vision. I wanna join you. I wanna come and play. I'm willing to put all my time and effort or my working life into helping drive that vision forward with you. So that's a little bit on team. It's been a big journey for me, but it's been super, super rewarding.
